questions.question: What is the correct syntax for a basic if statement in Java?
options: if condition { // code }
if (condition) { // code }
if [condition] { // code }
if {condition} ( // code )
questions.answer: if (condition) { // code }

questions.question: What type of expression must be placed inside the parentheses of an if statement?
options: An integer expression
A String expression
A boolean expression
Any expression that returns a value
questions.answer: A boolean expression

questions.question: What happens when the condition in an if statement evaluates to false and there is no else block?
options: The program throws a runtime error
The code inside the if block executes anyway
The program skips the if block and continues with the next statement
The program terminates immediately
questions.answer: The program skips the if block and continues with the next statement

questions.question: In an if-else statement, when does the else block execute?
options: When the if condition is true
When the if condition is false
Every time the program runs
Only when the if block throws an exception
questions.answer: When the if condition is false

questions.question: In an else-if chain, how does Java determine which block to execute?
options: It evaluates all conditions and executes all blocks where the condition is true
It evaluates conditions from top to bottom and executes the first block whose condition is true
It evaluates conditions from bottom to top and executes the last block whose condition is true
It randomly selects one of the true conditions to execute
questions.answer: It evaluates conditions from top to bottom and executes the first block whose condition is true

questions.question: What is the purpose of the else block at the end of an else-if chain?
options: It executes when the first condition is true
It acts as a default case when none of the previous conditions are true
It is required by the Java compiler
It executes after every other block has run
questions.answer: It acts as a default case when none of the previous conditions are true

options: Greater than 20
Hello
Both lines are printed
Nothing is printed
questions.answer: Nothing is printed

questions.question: In the 'dangling else' problem, which if statement does an else belong to when braces are omitted?
options: The first if statement in the chain
The outermost if statement
The nearest (innermost) if statement
It depends on the indentation of the code
questions.answer: The nearest (innermost) if statement

options: Nothing is printed
Excellent!
A compilation error occurs
A runtime error occurs
questions.answer: Excellent!

questions.question: Why does 'Excellent!' print in the code: if (score > 90); { System.out.println("Excellent!"); } when score is 85?
options: Because 85 is greater than 90
Because the semicolon after the if condition creates an empty if body, and the block below is independent code that always runs
Because the compiler ignores the if condition entirely
Because the curly braces override the if condition
questions.answer: Because the semicolon after the if condition creates an empty if body, and the block below is independent code that always runs

options: You can vote.

You can vote.
Welcome to adulthood.
Welcome to adulthood.
Nothing is printed
questions.answer: Welcome to adulthood.

questions.question: Why does 'Welcome to adulthood.' print in the previous question even though age is 15?
options: Because both println statements are inside the if block
Because without braces, only the first statement after the if is conditional; the second statement is outside the if and always executes
Because Java considers indentation when determining scope
Because the >= operator includes 15
questions.answer: Because without braces, only the first statement after the if is conditional; the second statement is outside the if and always executes

questions.question: What is a nested if statement?
options: An if statement that uses the && operator
An if statement placed inside another if statement's body
An if statement with multiple else-if clauses
An if statement that checks the same variable twice
questions.answer: An if statement placed inside another if statement's body

questions.question: Which of the following is generally equivalent to a nested if statement: if (a) { if (b) { // code } }?
options: if (a || b) { // code }
if (a && b) { // code }
if (a) else if (b) { // code }
if (!a && !b) { // code }
questions.answer: if (a && b) { // code }

questions.question: When should you use multiple separate if statements instead of an else-if chain?
options: When only one condition can be true at a time
When multiple conditions can be true simultaneously and all matching blocks should execute
When you want only the first true condition to execute
When the conditions are mutually exclusive
questions.answer: When multiple conditions can be true simultaneously and all matching blocks should execute

options: Only "Senior citizen"
"Can vote" and "Can rent a car" only
"Can vote", "Can rent a car", and "Senior citizen"
Only "Can vote"
questions.answer: "Can vote", "Can rent a car", and "Senior citizen"

questions.question: What does the method equalsIgnoreCase() do when comparing Strings in an if condition?
options: Compares two strings for equality, ignoring differences in uppercase and lowercase letters
Converts both strings to lowercase before the program runs
Throws an error if the strings have different cases
Checks if two string references point to the same object
questions.answer: Compares two strings for equality, ignoring differences in uppercase and lowercase letters

questions.question: Why is it best practice to always use curly braces with if statements, even for single-statement bodies?
options: The Java compiler requires them in all cases
It prevents bugs like the dangling else, accidental semicolons, and multi-statement traps
It makes the program run faster
It reduces the file size of the compiled class
questions.answer: It prevents bugs like the dangling else, accidental semicolons, and multi-statement traps

questions.question: What is a readability risk of deeply nested if statements?
options: They cause the program to run slower
They make the code harder to follow and maintain
They are not allowed by the Java compiler beyond 3 levels
They always produce incorrect results
questions.answer: They make the code harder to follow and maintain

options: A
B
C
D
questions.answer: B

questions.question: In the grading else-if chain, why does the order of conditions matter (e.g., checking >= 90 before >= 80)?
options: It does not matter; Java evaluates all conditions regardless of order
Because Java executes the first matching block and skips the rest, so more restrictive conditions must come first
Because the compiler sorts the conditions automatically
Because lower numbers must always be checked before higher numbers
questions.answer: Because Java executes the first matching block and skips the rest, so more restrictive conditions must come first

questions.question: When is nesting if statements preferable to combining conditions with the && operator?
options: Nesting is never preferable to using &&
When you need to perform different actions at each level of the decision or provide different error messages for each condition
When you want the code to be shorter
When the conditions are unrelated to each other
questions.answer: When you need to perform different actions at each level of the decision or provide different error messages for each condition

options: Nothing is printed
Heat warning!
A compilation error occurs because temp is not a boolean
Heat warning! is printed twice
questions.answer: Heat warning!

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a valid boolean expression for an if statement's condition?

options: Positive
Negative
Zero
Nothing is printed
questions.answer: Zero
